fields.summary: Introduced by 'דִּכְתִיב', this verse is cited as the original command of circumcision given to Noahides (Abraham and his descendants).
Introduced as repeated at Sinai, this verse is used to argue that circumcision was given only to Israel, not to Noahides.
Introduced by 'דִּכְתִיב', this verse is cited as the original command of procreation given to Noahides.

fields.excerpt: לֵךְ אֱמֹר לָהֶם שׁוּבוּ לָכֶם לְאׇהֳלֵיכֶם
fields.summary: Introduced as repeated at Sinai, this verse is used to argue that the command of procreation was given only to Israel, not to Noahides.

fields.excerpt: וְאַתָּה אֶת בְּרִיתִי תִשְׁמֹר אַתָּה וְזַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ לְדֹרֹתָם
fields.summary: This verse is cited to show that circumcision was commanded only to Abraham and his seed, not to others.
This verse is used to exclude Ishmael's descendants from the obligation of circumcision, as only Isaac is considered Abraham's seed.
This is a rabbinic interpretation of the verse, excluding Esau's descendants from the covenant.
This verse is cited to include the descendants of Keturah in the obligation of circumcision.

fields.excerpt: לָכֶם יִהְיֶה לְאׇכְלָה וּלְכׇל חַיַּת הָאָרֶץ
fields.summary: This verse is cited to prove that Adam was not permitted to eat meat, as the verse gives plants for food to both humans and animals.

fields.excerpt: כְּיֶרֶק עֵשֶׂב נָתַתִּי לָכֶם אֶת כֹּל
fields.summary: This verse is cited to show that meat was permitted to Noahides after the flood.

fields.excerpt: אַךְ בָּשָׂר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ
fields.summary: This verse is cited to derive the prohibition of eating a limb from a living animal (eiver min hachai).
This verse is cited in a challenge to prove that dominion over fish was for eating, but the response is that it was for labor.
This verse is cited similarly to the fish, to argue that dominion over birds was for eating, but the response is for labor.
This verse is cited to include the serpent in the dominion, but the Gemara explains it comes to teach about the serpent's role.
This verse is cited by Rabbi Shimon ben Chalafta when lions roared at him, and meat descended from heaven in response.
Introduced by 'דִּכְתִיב', this verse is cited as the original command of circumcision given to Noahides (Abraham and his descendants).
Introduced as repeated at Sinai, this verse is used to argue that circumcision was given only to Israel, not to Noahides.
